sentences.text: My full name is Andreas Chrambach, A-N-D-R-E-A-S, and the last name C-H-R-A-M-B-A-C-H.
The 23rd of April, 1927.
In Breslau.
That's in Silesia, Germany at that time.
Yes.
Richard Chrambach.
Richard, Richard Chrambach.
Annalise, nee [?
Friedmann. ?]
Yes, I have one brother, Max Chrambach.
Middle name is Carl, both for my brother and for me.
First four years of my life.
Yes, I know exactly the house, because I re-visited them.
Yes.
Yes.
It's Parkstrasse, or now Parkova.
Number 1 for my grandparents, and I think my parents' house is number 3.
It's the adjacent house.
I visited Breslau, Vrotslav now, a couple of years ago.
Yes.
I saw the house of my birth, also the house of my grandparents again.
Well, yes.
I mean, the house of my grandparents is sort of a luxurious palace.
It's unbelievable, beautiful place.
And my parents' house is a modest wood structure, sort of a country house, made of wood, very pretty, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and much smaller, of course, than my grandfather's.
Breslau must have roughly 3/4 of a million or 1 million inhabitants.
It's the capital of Silesia.
Well, certainly-- my parents were married in 1924.
So they have been certainly living in Breslau together since then.
I think my father spent his entire previous life in Breslau, apart from the war years.
Yes.
Yes.
My grandparents, my paternal grandparents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lived in Breslau nearly all of their lives.

That means my grandfather came to Breslau as a very young man, as a boy, I understand 14 or 15 years old, from a little place close to Posen-- it's now Poznan, called Wisla, and-- and started working in a bank at age 15, I think, in Breslau, then stayed there over-- over his whole entire life, became a very wealthy banker and industrialist.
That's my paternal grandfather.
His name was Carl Chrambach.
Yes.
He always had jobs.
I mean, his first job starting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe two or three years prior to World War I was to become a professional German soldier.
And at the end of World War I, he emerged from this as-- with a rank above lieutenant and below captain.
I don't know the English expression for it.
But he quit the army with-- when the Republic was created in Germany.
And then after spent I think a couple of years in what is called in German the Freikorps.
Freikorps were right-wing groups, mostly made up of old army soldiers who were fighting Communists in the streets of Germany for control of the country.
And when he-- when that came to an end with the firm establishment of the Social Democratic government and liberal Weimar Republic, he became a banker.
In fact, he with a colleague, I think, were acquired a bank, a private bank, I suspect with the good offices of my grandfather.
And he was a banker until the world bank crash in 1929 or possibly '31.
I'm not sure about that.
I think it's 29.
And then he-- he became the chief administrator in a compound of industrial and agricultural estates that belonged to a Prince Hohenlohe, Hohenlohe [INAUDIBLE].

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in many ways, this job, the way I think it was, was that of the-- of the [INAUDIBLE] in the courts of the Austrian Emperors, the guys who were responsible for the financial well-beings of the-- of the entire thing.

And so he was and he was very successful at it, at, I think, bringing this economic monster to fruition, to life, so much so that to the present day the Prince Hohenlohe administration sends my mother a small check every month in appreciation of what my father did for them.
And that went well until 1936, when he had to leave the country.
Apparently he was forewarned of an arrest at that time.
That was when I was-- when I was 9 years old.
Nine years old he left the family-- and we were living in Berlin at that time-- and went to Belgium and to-- ultimately to Hungary.
Four years.
Well, we-- OK.

Undoubtedly what prompted us to move away was the end of the bank career of my father's and the beginning of his career with the Hohenlohes, because the place where we moved to, Slawentzitz, was the main castle of Prince Hohenlohe and the seat of much of his administration.
But we only stayed there for one year.
That was memorable for me only because I started school, primary school,
Yes.
In Slawentzitz.
I can.
S-L-A-W-E-N-T-Z-I-T-Z, in Upper Silesia.
No, not really.
No.
No.
No.
My memories are mostly associated with a place we moved then, after one year in Slawentzitz, which was Berlin, where I went to primary school and started high school.
And that, of course, not only I remember, but has remained sort of home for me, one of the many places I call home.
Yes, very well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived in the famous Bendlerstrasse, which was the next door from the War Ministry, which was-- became world famous as the seat of the conspiracy against fascism in 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And it's a-- it's a beautiful neighborhood.
Well, [SIGHING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] only to say that, you know, it was-- it's a-- it's a Victorian structure.
It was a Victorian structure.
Of course, it has not survived the war.

It was huge, tall rooms, and such large-- large apartment-- apartments on each floor that we actually, my brother and I and a governess, practically never saw our parents who lived on the other side of the apartment.
I mean, these were huge places.
And it was-- was very, very luxurious and very nice.
Until-- until we-- we had to move in 1938.
This was, of course, after my father was gone already.
A law came out that Jews were not allowed to employ domestics.
And since we had three of those, then we moved in a very small place, the street of which I don't remember.
But it was very close to the KaDeWe, the famous--
Yes.
Yes.
My-- my parents were-- were very wealthy at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes.
In Berlin?
Well, the-- I went to three years of primary school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember the teacher used to have his World War I uniform hanging on the walls and definitely prided himself in maintaining military discipline and language in class.
He also made considerable use of a stick to hit us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these were obviously years of extreme well-being and happiness for us children.
Well, some crazy things come back to my mind.
For instance, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] our upbringing was rather militaristic, as you can imagine, with the background of my father as by his choice a professional soldier.
And he always really maintained the-- the attitudes and the allures of a soldier.
So needless to say, we picked that up and our main toys were-- had to do with soldiery.

So we had whole armies built up on the floor in our rooms, and shot at these was little canons that had pencils, you know, preferably shooting off the heads of these little [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] soldiers.
I mean, they were made of not plastic.
This preceded plastic.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Plaster?
It was plaster probably, yes.
Yes.
Anyway, that was one of the things.
And then we-- we also dressed ourselves in uniforms with sabers and whatnot and battled with our friends, preferably with the lights out.
Surprisingly, everybody survived that with all the eyes in a place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we were fanatics about going to the zoo, and discovered that the favorite food of monkeys were live meal worms.
So we-- we went sort of every-- every afternoon to-- or whenever we could with our governess to the-- to the zoo, bringing bags full of-- of live meal worms.

And so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it must have been 1936 or possibly 1937, when-- suddenly when my mother asked us one day into her salon in the same place, in Bendlerstrasse-- my father was gone already-- and told us that we were legal Jews, which obviously at that time, even for a nine-year-old, meant that we were second-class citizens.
And-- and in fact, our ethnic identity as Germans was denied to us.
Oh, yeah.
I was-- both of us children were baptized within a week of our birth as Roman Catholics, since both of my parents were practicing and actually rather religious Roman Catholics.
My father, I think, since I suspect he was baptized with his brother-- together with his brother Fritz in Budapest when he was 12 or 13.
He was a young teenager.
And my mother was baptized in 1932 after I was born, five years after I was born.
But she is certainly the one who is the most religious.
To the present day, I mean, she is a daily church-goer, to the present day, while I have abandoned religion entirely since I'm in my early 20s.
No.
I don't think so--
No.
I don't think so.
In fact, I think religion in prison and in concentration camp probably helped me a good deal to survive.
I mean, I remember kneeling and praying with my brother in prison every night.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I remember at the very first-- the first weekend after liberation by the Red Army in Auschwitz, I walked something like 10 kilometers to the next church to attend mass.
So I was quite religious.
At the time it has helped me.
No, the-- you know, my-- my shedding of religion has really much to do with my intellectual development later.
And I think hasn't been final until I was, what, 20, 21, must be 22.
Yeah.
Well, it's not that I considered myself second-class citizen.
In fact, you know, very emphatically we never did, refused to accept that.

But it's fascist society that treated us like second-class citizens in-- in many simple ways, you know, from not being allowed to go to the theater, having to sit on yellow benches in the parks, ultimately, something we actually never did, wearing a yellow star on our clothes.
But you know, it's obvious that apart from the-- the killing off of Jews in the later course of the war, ever since 1936 Jews were considered [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] second or third-class.
[INAUDIBLE] Just to give you an example, in the parks of Berlin there were signs put up which said, no dogs or Jews, in that order.
So even when you're a 9- or 10-year-old, there was no ambiguity about what that meant to be a Jew in that country.
Not one.
Well, not true.
In Budapest.
In Budapest, there were Jewish classmates I had, yes.
But these were Hungarians, you see, so German school didn't worry about this.
As far as they're concerned, they were Hungarians.
No.
Not in Berlin.
No.
It was not-- well, you know, not in-- not in grammar school.
Obviously, not in high school, because it was run by the Jesuits.
At the same moment.
Well-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] well, we reacted it to it, I think, in two ways.
One, being depressed about refusal of that society to accept us as equals.
And another one-- another one was the attitude of never accepting that division.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So it was refusal as well as-- as-- as anger.
Yeah.
Very much so.
As I said, we had to move out of our luxurious dwellings and to a much more modest place that my mother could administer by herself.
And of course, I was excluded from going to-- to go to high school.

It was just through the helpfulness of the Jesuits that-- that they allowed me to-- to pass-- to enter their school, which was being abolished into second class of high school into the first, which is why I always went to the same class with my brother.
No, none whatsoever.
None whatsoever.
The regime was totally uninterested in people's religion.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our status was defined by the Nuremberg laws, nothing else.
Well, the-- the legislation that didn't allows us to have servants.
Well, it was enforced [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like all laws are enforced.
I mean, once you say that no-- no domestic can work in a Jewish household, domestics had to leave.
So I don't think there was a problem of enforcement.
Well, yes.
I mean, well, this is even much more so the case in Germany than it's in this country.
You know, if there's a law promulgated, it is instantly obeyed by everybody.
That's what people are educated to do.
No.
He had-- he already was gone since 1936.
Yes.
Yes.
I mean, he was not-- his life-- I understand he was in fear of imprisonment in 1936.
And I don't know who forewarned him, but I suspect the same people we will be talking about later, who led to his employment by the-- by the German army in later course.
I suspect it was the same people who got a whiff of his pending arrest.
No.
I don't.
No.
That one I don't know at all.

I can only speculate that possibly it also had to do with the fact that he started, which must have been around shortly before he left, maybe 1935, a process at the-- what's called the Reich [INAUDIBLE], the office that had to do with family structure, started a process that was called Arisierung, or Aryanization, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the hope to escape the anti-Semitic persecution for himself and his family that way.
And that case was built on the-- on the notion that he wasn't at all the child of my grandparents, but that his real father was a Hungarian general by the name of [?
Erdent von Banitsky, ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who, so goes the saga, stayed some time in the house of my grandparents in Breslau and conceived and-- and-- and-- and was really Father's father.
Now, that-- that story [LAUGHS] was corroborated by a funny accident apparently.

And this is that the skull measurements, on which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these cases were usually determined at that time, lacking the chemistry of today-- skull measurements were identical apparently, this Hungarian general and-- and my father.
And how they ever figured out, I don't know.
But so I suspect that the arrest probably had to do with the fact that this was such an obvious-- obvious lie.
Probably somebody thought so.
That probably had to do with it.
But that's pure speculation.
I really don't know that.
Fact is that he left.
He lived in Belgium.
He lived in Hungary.
And he provided us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at one point with Hungarian passports, my mother and my brother and I.
That's where he saved our lives.
Well, then finally Mother understood what was going on.
In the picture books, the Hungarian passports.
Lost their validity, I think, three days later.
So just before they had elapsed, we took these things and a few a suitcases, met [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] General [?
Banitsky ?] in Vienna, and then took the train to Budapest.

Obviously, these passports were also obviously fishy, because we didn't speak a word of Hungarian and also didn't have the financial stamps that were needed at that time, just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the amount of currency that's allowed to take out.
They had absolutely nothing there.
And it took considerable talking of-- of the general to the Hungarian officials in the train to allow the-- to allow us to pass.
